title: An efficient Wavelet-Based Hamiltonian Formulation of Quantum Field Theories using Flow-Equations
summary: We propose an effective Hamiltonian formulation of quantum field theories using a Daubechies wavelet basis in position space. Combined with flow-equation methods of the similarity renormalization group (SRG), this approach provides an efficient framework for analyzing quantum field theories by reducing the dimensionality of the Hamiltonian and systematically decoupling degrees of freedom across scales. As an application, the free scalar field theory has been reformulated within this framework to calculate the low-lying energy spectrum of the theory. These basis elements are known to transform the free scalar field theory into a theory of coupled localized oscillators, each of which is labeled by a location and a resolution index. In this representation, the Hamiltonian is naturally organized into fixed-resolution blocks, alongside blocks associated with the interactions between different resolutions. To decouple the different resolution modes and obtain a block diagonalized Hamiltonian with each block associated with a fixed resolution, the flow equation approach of SRG is applied. Finally, we demonstrate that with increasing resolution, the low-energy spectrum can be extracted from the effective lowest-resolution block of the Hamiltonian, leading to a significant reduction in computational cost.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a new approach to quantum field theories using Daubechies wavelets and SRG, which simplifies the Hamiltonian and allows for efficient computation of the low-lying energy spectrum, with a focus on free scalar field theory.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to provide an efficient framework for analyzing quantum field theories by reducing the dimensionality of the Hamiltonian and decoupling degrees of freedom across scales, which can lead to a significant reduction in computational cost.
AI.method: The method involves reformulating the free scalar field theory using a Daubechies wavelet basis and applying the flow-equation methods of the similarity renormalization group (SRG) to decouple different resolution modes and block diagonalize the Hamiltonian.
AI.result: The result is that the low-energy spectrum can be extracted from the effective lowest-resolution block of the Hamiltonian, as the resolution increases, leading to a more computationally efficient way to analyze the theory.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that the proposed wavelet-based formulation, combined with SRG, provides an efficient and systematic way to analyze quantum field theories, particularly for computing the low-lying energy spectrum of a free scalar field theory.

title: A minimal implementation of Yang-Mills theory on a digital quantum computer
summary: We present a minimal implementation of SU($N$) pure Yang-Mills theory in $3+1$ dimensions for digital quantum simulation, designed to enable quantum advantage. Building on the orbifold lattice simulation protocol with logarithmic scaling in the local Hilbert-space truncation, we introduce further simplified Hamiltonians. Furthermore, we test simple methods that improve the convergence to the infinite mass limit, thereby removing the requirement of a large scalar mass to obtain the Kogut-Susskind Hamiltonian. For the SU(2) theory, we can cut the resource requirement further by utilizing the embedding of $\mathrm{SU}(2)\cong\mathrm{S}^3$ into $\mathbb{R}^4$. Monte Carlo simulations of the Euclidean path integral were used to benchmark the accuracy of these new analytical improvements to the theory. These results provide further support for the noncompact-variable-based approach as a practical framework for quantum simulation of non-Abelian gauge theories.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a minimal implementation of SU(N) pure Yang-Mills theory in 3+1 dimensions, optimized for digital quantum simulation. The authors introduce simplified Hamiltonians and methods to improve convergence, reducing the need for large scalar masses. For SU(2), they use an embedding into R^4 to further reduce resource requirements. Monte Carlo simulations validate the accuracy of these improvements, supporting the noncompact-variable-based approach for simulating non-Abelian gauge theories.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to develop a more efficient and practical framework for the digital quantum simulation of SU(N) pure Yang-Mills theory, with the aim of achieving quantum advantage by reducing computational resources and improving the convergence to the infinite mass limit.
AI.method: The method involves implementing a minimal version of the orbifold lattice simulation protocol, introducing further simplified Hamiltonians, and testing simple methods that enhance convergence towards the Kogut-Susskind Hamiltonian. Additionally, for the SU(2) case, they exploit the topological embedding of S^3 into R^4 to minimize resource usage. The accuracy of the new analytical improvements is benchmarked using Monte Carlo simulations of the Euclidean path integral.
AI.result: The results show that the introduced simplifications and convergence improvement methods are effective, as validated through Monte Carlo simulations. This supports the viability of the noncompact-variable-based approach for quantum simulation of non-Abelian gauge theories.
AI.conclusion: The conclusion is that the proposed modifications to the orbifold lattice simulation protocol and the introduction of new techniques for better convergence provide a practical and resource-efficient way to simulate non-Abelian gauge theories, such as SU(N) pure Yang-Mills, on a quantum computer, potentially leading to quantum advantage.

title: Charmonium radiative transitions to dileptons from lattice QCD: The case of $h_c \to η_c \ell^+\ell^-$ and $χ_{c1} \to J/ψ\,\ell^+\ell^-$
summary: We present a lattice QCD study of dilepton production in charmonium transitions, specifically focusing on the $1^{+-} \to 0^{-+}$ and $1^{++} \to 1^{--}$ processes: $h_c \to η_c \ell^+ \ell^-$ and $χ_{c1} \to J/ψ\ell^+ \ell^-$, where $\ell = e, μ$. The relevant hadronic matrix elements are computed using gauge field configurations generated by the Extended Twisted Mass Collaboration with $N_f = 2+1+1$ dynamical Wilson--Clover twisted-mass fermions at four lattice spacings. Simulations are performed at physical dynamical $u$, $d$, $s$, and $c$ quark masses, except for the coarsest lattice, where the lightest sea quark mass corresponds to a slightly heavier pion mass. A controlled continuum extrapolation is carried out. In the continuum limit for the $h_c$ decays, we obtain $Γ(h_c \to η_c e^+ e^-) = 5.45(19)~\mathrm{keV}$, and $Γ(h_c \to η_c μ^+ μ^-) = 0.635(22)~\mathrm{keV}$. For the $χ_{c1}$ decays, we find: $Γ(χ_{c1} \to J/ψe^+ e^-)= 2.869(90)~\mathrm{keV}$, and $Γ(χ_{c1} \to J/ψμ^+ μ^-) = 0.1993(72)~\mathrm{keV}$. Our results for the $χ_{c1}$ decays show good compatibility with experimental data. However, our prediction for the $h_c \to η_c e^+ e^- $ decay rate is approximately $3σ$ larger than the BESIII result. We also present predictions for the differential decay widths as functions of the dilepton invariant mass, $q^2$, and for angular observables sensitive to longitudinal transition form factors, which are inaccessible in radiative decays with real photon emission. These results constitute the first fully dynamical lattice QCD predictions for dilepton decay rates in $h_c$ and $χ_{c1}$ charmonium transitions, including their differential distributions and angular observables. They provide benchmark predictions for future experimental studies.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a lattice QCD study of dilepton production in charmonium transitions, providing the first fully dynamical predictions for the decay rates, differential distributions, and angular observables. The results are compared with experimental data, showing good agreement for χc1 decays but a discrepancy for the hc to ηc e+ e- decay rate.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to provide accurate theoretical predictions for the processes of h_c to η_c ℓ^+ ℓ^- and χ_{c1} to J/ψℓ^+ ℓ^- using lattice Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD), which can be used as benchmarks for future experimental studies and to test the Standard Model.
AI.method: The authors use gauge field configurations generated by the Extended Twisted Mass Collaboration with N_f = 2+1+1 dynamical Wilson--Clover twisted-mass fermions at four different lattice spacings. They perform simulations at physical quark masses, except on the coarsest lattice, and carry out a controlled continuum extrapolation to obtain the decay rates and related observables.
AI.result: The study yields Γ(h_c to η_c e^+ e^-) = 5.45(19) keV, Γ(h_c to η_c μ^+ μ^-) = 0.635(22) keV, Γ(χ_{c1} to J/ψe^+ e^-) = 2.869(90) keV, and Γ(χ_{c1} to J/ψμ^+ μ^-) = 0.1993(72) keV. The χ_{c1} results agree well with experiments, while the h_c to η_c e^+ e^- prediction is larger than the BESIII result. Differential decay widths and angular observables are also predicted.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes with the first fully dynamical lattice QCD predictions for dilepton decay rates in h_c and χ_{c1} charmonium transitions, including their differential distributions and angular observables, which can serve as reference points for upcoming experimental analyses.
